sections.heading: Demand barely changed, but modern methods covered more of it
sections.body: Among all women aged 15 to 49, total demand for family planning was 41.4 percent in 2025, almost the same as 41.8 percent in 2022. Met need through all methods also barely changed, at 33.8 percent in 2025 compared with 34.1 percent in 2022. The bigger shift was within met need: modern methods rose from 24.3 percent to 25.7 percent, while unmet need stayed almost the same at 7.6 percent.

sections.heading: Demand is highest among women in the middle age groups
sections.body: Total demand rises sharply after the teenage years, from 6.0 percent at ages 15 to 19 to 46.6 percent at ages 25 to 29. It reaches its highest point among women aged 35 to 39, at 63.0 percent. Met need through all methods is also highest in that group, at 53.9 percent. Unmet need varies less across the main reproductive ages, from 7.1 percent at ages 45 to 49 to 10.3 percent at ages 25 to 29.

sections.heading: Rural women had higher demand, but the gap was not the biggest divide
sections.body: In 2025, rural women had higher total demand than urban women, at 44.4 percent compared with 39.1 percent. Rural women also had higher met need through all methods, at 36.3 percent compared with 31.9 percent, and higher modern-method met need, at 28.8 percent compared with 23.3 percent. Unmet need was closer between the two groups, at 8.1 percent in rural areas and 7.2 percent in urban areas.

sections.heading: Demand does not rise or fall neatly with education
sections.body: Family planning demand does not simply go up with more schooling or down with less schooling. In 2025, total demand was highest among women with Grades 1 to 6 education, at 59.6 percent, and among women with post-secondary education, at 56.1 percent. It was much lower among women with Grades 11 to 12, at 20.4 percent. Unmet need was highest among women with no education, at 13.9 percent, and Grades 1 to 6, at 11.7 percent, even though those two groups had very different levels of met need.

sections.heading: Poorer groups had higher demand and higher unmet need
sections.body: Total demand was highest in the lowest wealth quintile, at 50.7 percent, and declined to 34.5 percent in the highest quintile. Met need through all methods moved in the same general direction, from 39.6 percent in the lowest quintile to 28.4 percent in the highest. Unmet need was also higher at the bottom of the wealth distribution, at 11.2 percent in the lowest quintile compared with 6.1 percent in the highest.

sections.heading: BARMM stood out most among regions
sections.body: In 2025, BARMM had the lowest met need through all methods, at 16.2 percent, and the highest unmet need, at 18.5 percent. Its total demand was 34.8 percent, close to several other regions with lower demand, but its split between met and unmet need was very different. Davao Region showed the opposite pattern, with the highest met need through all methods at 45.1 percent and much lower unmet need at 6.2 percent.

sections.heading: Several regions were far ahead on modern-method met need
sections.body: In 2025, Davao Region had the highest modern-method met need, at 37.1 percent. It was followed by MIMAROPA at 33.9 percent, SOCCSKSARGEN at 32.5 percent, and Caraga at 32.2 percent. All of these regions were well above the national modern-method level of 25.7 percent. BARMM was at the other end, at 13.0 percent, so the regional spread in modern-method met need was larger than the rural-urban gap.

sections.heading: Some regions changed even though the national picture was stable
sections.body: National total demand changed little between 2022 and 2025, but several regions moved differently. Central Visayas fell from 45.9 percent to 35.5 percent in total demand, while its met need through all methods dropped from 37.0 percent to 25.7 percent. Eastern Visayas moved in the opposite direction, with total demand rising from 41.3 percent to 46.8 percent and met need through all methods rising from 35.6 percent to 39.7 percent.

sections.heading: Zamboanga Peninsula had higher unmet need, even with average overall demand
sections.body: Zamboanga Peninsula¹ had unmet need of 11.0 percent in 2025, higher than the national level of 7.6 percent. Its met need through all methods was 30.2 percent, below the national level of 33.8 percent, while total demand was close to the national level at 41.2 percent. This points to a region where overall demand was not unusually high, but a larger share of that demand was still unmet.

takeaways: National family planning demand among all women stayed stable, but modern methods accounted for more of the met need in 2025.
Differences between regions are larger than the rural-urban divide, especially for modern-method met need and unmet need.
BARMM stands out because unmet need is high while met need is low, which is different from regions where demand is simply lower.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
